extra.reasoning:
The variable abdominal_pain appears in exactly one rule, r10 (priority 100, the highest priority by far), which fires only when all ten of {vomiting, stiff_neck, sweating, back_pain, pallor, dizziness, leg_swelling, slurred_speech, chest_pain, abdominal_pain} are true and sets acuity to self_care, the LOWEST acuity level. This creates a non-monotone interaction.

Counterexample (the witness shows the abdominal_pain=true state; compare against the same inputs with abdominal_pain=false):

BEFORE (abdominal_pain = false, all other listed inputs true, fever false): r10 does not fire because abdominal_pain is false. The matching rules among r1-r9,r11 include r2 (slurred_speech, prio 3), r5 (back_pain, prio 3), r8 (stiff_neck, prio 3), r11 (dizziness, prio 3) all setting emergent, plus several priority-2 urgent rules. The highest-priority matches are the priority-3 emergent rules, so acuity = emergent.

AFTER (abdominal_pain = true, everything else identical): now all ten conditions of r10 are satisfied, so r10 fires with priority 100, which exceeds every other rule's priority (max 3). r10 wins and sets acuity = self_care.

Setting abdominal_pain from false to true lowers acuity from emergent to self_care (a strict decrease in the listed enum order self_care < routine < urgent < emergent). This directly violates the monotonicity property that setting abdominal_pain true must never lower acuity. (fever can be true or false; false is chosen for concreteness — even with fever true the before-state is emergent and the after-state is self_care, since r10's priority 100 dominates.)
